segments.s486-g57: گزین کروکرد is a two-word verbal phrase: گزین (choose/select) followed by کرد, here the past-tense auxiliary 'did' marking a completed action. The preposition ز (poetic 'from') introduces the source (ایرانیان). Word order is verb + object/source, a common poetic order where the verb precedes its object.
segments.s486-g58: که begins a relative clause 'who/that'; بودند is the past third-person plural of 'to be' and links the clause to ایرانیان. با + آلت کارزار functions as an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with the instruments of battle' (i.e. equipped for war). The clause describes those thirty thousand.
segments.s486-g59: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/advanced' (here used narratively). کارآزموده is a preposed adjective meaning 'seasoned/experienced' modifying سران. The noun سران ('leaders') follows the adjective in this poetic ordering, so the phrase reads 'the seasoned leaders went forth.'
segments.s486-g60: This cluster lists attributes/appellations: زره‌دار functions as an adjective 'armored' (or 'armored ones'), followed by مردان 'men' and جنگاوران 'warriors/fighters' in apposition; the nouns amplify the kind of leaders just mentioned. The absence of explicit conjunctions is typical of epic catalogue style.
segments.s486-g61: پس 'then' signals sequence; پشت ایشان 'behind them' places the following forces in the rear. ز before رومی indicates origin/source 'from the Romans', and سوار is a noun 'horseman' (here plural sense implied by the continuation). Word order places the temporal/positional phrase before the identifying noun.
segments.s486-g62: این گروه is a brief enumerative clause: یکی 'one' begins, قلب here means 'core/contingent' (used poetically as a military unit), دیگر 'another', and همان چل 'the same forty' qualifies the number; the sense is 'one unit, another, the same forty thousand' — a compressed way to state repeated corps totaling forty thousand. The elliptical syntax (missing explicit 'هزار' in the provided words) is typical of epic meter.
segments.s486-g63: پس 'then' + پشت ایشان 'behind them' repeats the sequence marker and position. سواران مصر is a noun phrase 'the horsemen of Egypt' (سواران = riders/horsemen, مصر = Egypt), with the genitive relationship implied by adjacency typical in Persian epic diction.
segments.s486-g64: This is a nominal apposition listing qualities/groups: دلیران 'the brave' and خنجرگزاران 'the dagger-bearers' (those who carry or wield daggers), followed by مصر again to mark their origin. The coordinating و links the two epithets; the phrase further specifies the Egyptian horsemen mentioned just before.
segments.s486-g1: 'چو' is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a temporal clause. The clause follows a common Persian order here with the verb 'رسید' (past 3rd sg.) at the end: 'پاسخ ... رسید' = 'the reply reached'. 'به نزد سکندر' is a directional/locative prepositional phrase ('to/into the presence of Alexander'), where 'نزد' = 'in the presence of/near'.
segments.s486-g2: 'هم‌انگه' is an adverb meaning 'at that instant/thereupon'. 'ز لشکر سران' is a prepositional phrase 'from the army, (the) leaders' (z = 'from'), and 'برگزید' is past tense 3rd singular 'chose/selected'. The subject is omitted (understood from context). Word order is typical poetic/older Persian: adverb + prepositional phrase + verb.
segments.s486-g3: 'که' here introduces a relative/result clause meaning 'so that' / 'who'. 'باشند' is 3rd person plural present-subjunctive/optative used after 'که' to express wished-for or intended qualities ('may be' / 'should be'); it agrees with the plural 'سران' (leaders). 'شایسته و پیش‌رو' are predicate adjectives: 'worthy and foremost'.
segments.s486-g4: This is a participial/ adjectival phrase describing the leaders. 'به دانش کهن گشته' uses 'گشته' (become/turned/become versed) to mean 'having become skilled in ancient knowledge' (the preposition 'به' marks the domain: 'in/with knowledge'). The following 'و سال نو' is coordinated to the previous phrase and is elliptical: it attributes 'نو' (new) to 'سال' (years/age) — i.e. 'and (also) new in years' or 'young' — so the clause as a whole qualifies the chosen leaders. Grammatically, 'گشته' links back to the subjunctive 'باشند' earlier (they should be ... having become ...).
segments.s486-g5: 'سوی' is the preposition 'toward(s)'. 'فور' here is an adverb 'immediately/at once' placed after the preposition (a poetic placement). 'هندی سپاهی' is the noun phrase 'Indian army' (adjective + noun). 'براند' is an older/poetic past form 'drove/sent/led' (3rd person singular), meaning 'he sent/led (them)'.
segments.s486-g6: 'که' introduces a result or explanatory clause here ('so that/with the result that'). 'روی زمین' = 'on the surface of the earth' (location). 'جز به دریا نماند' is a negative-except construction: 'جز' = 'except', 'به دریا' marks what is excepted ('except the sea'), and 'نماند' (past/perfect 3rd sg.) means 'remained' / 'was left' — together: 'there remained nothing on the land except the sea.' The clause states the result of sending the army.
segments.s486-g7: 'به هر سو' = 'to every direction/side'. 'همی رفت' combines the archaic progressive particle 'همی' with the past form 'رفت' to give an ongoing past sense 'was going/kept going'. 'زان‌سان سپاه' places 'زان‌سان' ('thus/so') before the subject 'سپاه' ('the army'), yielding 'the army thus went in every direction.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s486-g8: 'تو گفتی' is the main clause 'you said' (second person past; note: the provided sense list for 'گفتی' did not include 'said', so the exact dictionary sense is not present). The remainder 'جز آن بر زمین نیست راه' is a nominal negative-existential statement with 'جز' fronted: literally 'there is no way on the earth except that one.' 'بر زمین' = 'on the land/ground', 'نیست' = 'is not/does not exist', and 'راه' = 'path/way'. Word order places the exception 'جز آن' up front for emphasis.
segments.s486-g65: برفتند is the past plural of رفتن with the prefix بر- giving the sense 'went forth/advanced'; the verb appears before its noun phrase, a common poetic inversion. "شمشیرزن چل هزار" is a quantified noun phrase meaning 'forty thousand swordsmen' (شمشیرزن 'swordsman' followed by the numeral چل هزار 'forty thousand').
segments.s486-g66: هرانکس 'whoever' begins a relative clause; که is the relative particle 'who/that' linking to the verb بود (past 'was'). از introduces the source/partitive phrase and در here functions as 'gate/realm/place' of action in a poetic sense, so از در کارزار means 'from the gate/realm of battle' — i.e. 'belonging to the battlefield.'
segments.s486-g67: ز (poetic) = 'from', followed by خویشان 'kinsmen/relatives'. دارا here refers to the named figure 'Dara' in genitive relation to خویشان (i.e. 'Dara's relatives'), and و ایرانیان adds 'and the Iranians' as a coordinated noun phrase.
segments.s486-g68: This repeats the relative-clause pattern: هرانکس 'whoever' + که 'who/that' + بود past 'was'. از marks origin/lineage and نژاد کیان means 'the race/lineage of the Kian (royal) house' — 'of the Kian line.'
segments.s486-g69: This is a coordinated list introduced by the poetic preposition ز 'from' (then repeated as از): رومی 'Rûm/Byzantine (Roman)', و 'and', از 'from', مصری 'Egyptian', و بربری 'and Berber/barbarian'. The repeated prepositions are a poetic listing device.
segments.s486-g70: سواران 'riders/horsemen' is the head noun, modified by شایسته 'worthy' and then coordinated with لشکری 'troops/soldiery' using و 'and'. The phrase describes the quality and type of the forces drawn from those peoples.
segments.s486-g71: گزین کرد is the compound past 'chose/selected' (گزین + کرد), with the subject following the verb: قیصر 'the Caesar' (subject) and the object/quantity 'ده و دو هزار' ('ten and two thousand' = twelve thousand). The numeral phrase follows the subject in this poetic word order.
segments.s486-g72: همه ... و همه ... is a parallel structure for emphasis: 'all ... and all ...'. رزمجوی here means 'combatant/warrior' and نامدار 'renowned/famed', so the line lists the character of the chosen twelve thousand as every warrior and every famous man.
segments.s486-g9: This is a coordinated noun phrase: 'همه' (quantifier) scopes over the list 'کوه و دریا و راه درشت' — 'every/ all mountain and sea and (the) broad road'. Word order is literal: items joined by و. The adjective 'درشت' follows and modifies 'راه' (i.e. 'broad/large road').
segments.s486-g10: This clause expresses the effect: 'به دل' = 'into/onto the heart(s)'; 'آتش جنگ‌جویان' is a genitive phrase (the ezafe is omitted in elevated/poetic word order) meaning 'the fire of the warriors' (i.e. the warriors' burning ardor); 'بکشت' is an archaic past-form verb with the b- prefix used in classical Persian — here it functions as the main predicative verb ('laid waste/overwhelmed', literal lemma given as an archaic 'do/make'). The subject is omitted (carried over from context) so the whole reads 'the warriors' fire laid waste (to) in the heart(s)'.
segments.s486-g11: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'; 'رفتن' is a verbal noun here ('the going/ departure/ marching'); 'سپه' is the noun 'army' governed by that preposition. 'سربسر گشت کند' is a predicate: 'سربسر' ('everywhere/entirely') modifies the verb phrase, 'گشت' = 'became', and 'کند' is used adjectivally/predicatively here ('slow/feeble' → 'grew weary/sluggish'). Together: 'Because of the army's departure, the host everywhere became weary.'
segments.s486-g12: 'ازان' = 'from that / from it' (referring back to the road/march); 'راه دشوار' = 'difficult road' (adjective after noun as usual); 'و پیکار تند' = 'and the fierce/rapid fighting' where 'تند' modifies 'پیکار'. This phrase gives the cause/grounding of the earlier fatigue ('from that difficult road and the fierce fighting').
segments.s486-g13: هم‌انگه چو آمد به منزل سپاه is an adverbial subordinate clause meaning 'when/at the moment the army arrived at the station.' هم‌انگه (همان‌گاه) is an adverb 'at that moment.' چو is the archaic subordinating conjunction 'when' placed before آمد (past tense, 3rd singular of آمدن). به منزل سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the station/encampment of the army' (منزل here = stopping-place/stage).
segments.s486-g14: گروهی برفتند نزدیک شاه is the main clause: گروهی ('a group' / 'some of them') is an indefinite plural subject/agent, برفتند is an archaic or emphatic past plural verb (برفتند ~ رفتند) meaning 'they went / advanced,' and نزدیک شاه is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'near the king' indicating direction/proximity. Word order here is typical V-final in Persian but with the adverbial following the verb for emphasis.
segments.s486-g15: که‌ای قیصر روم و سالار چین is a vocative/title phrase addressing someone with two epithets. که‌ای is an archaic poetic form equivalent to ای که / ای (vocative 'O'), here introducing the address; قیصر روم = 'Caesar of Rome' and سالار چین = 'commander/lord of China.' The coordinating و links the two titles.
segments.s486-g16: سپاه ترا برنتابد زمین is a terse, poetic clause meaning 'may the earth not endure/tolerate your army.' سپاه + ت (سپاهت → 'your army') shows the possessive clitic ت separated in writing; را is the object/definiteness marker attached after the possessive clitic. برنتابد is an archaic/fused negative jussive/present form meaning 'may not bear/tolerate' (a curse-like optative), and زمین ('the earth') is placed after the verb for emphasis, so the literal order here is object–verb–subject.
segments.s486-g89: سواری (rider) is the subject/noun. نیارد here expresses inability of anyone to come up to or mount him (a negative or prohibitive sense); بر او شدن is a light verbal construction meaning “to be upon him / to attack/become situated on him.” Word order is noun + verb + prepositional complement, typical of classical Persian. "شدن" in this phrase functions as the verb “to become / to come to pass” linked to بر او (to be upon him).
segments.s486-g90: نه introduces a negative coordination (“nor / not”); چون شد بود is a compact past (pluperfect-like) expression: شد (happened/occurred) plus بود as an auxiliary yields “when/after it had happened.” راه is the noun “way/path,” and بازآمدن is the infinitive “to come back/return.” Together the clause means “nor, when that happened, was there a way to return.”
segments.s486-g91: که is the subordinating conjunction “for/that.” خرطوم او is the noun phrase “his trunk.” از هوا برترست is a predicate: از هوا (“above/from the air”) + برترست, an archaic/poetic third‑person singular predicate meaning “is higher/surpasses.” Word order places the comparative predicate after its phrase, typical in classical verse.
segments.s486-g92: ز گردون is a poetic locative “from the heavens/sky.” مر is the dative/beneficiary pronoun “to me.” او را marks “him” as a (topical/definite) object; zحل (Saturn) is the agent and یاورست is the third‑person singular predicative “is a helper/ally.” The unusual order (benefactive before object and then the agent) is ornamental: literally, “From the sky, to me, he (i.e. Saturn) is his helper.”
segments.s486-g93: به قرطاس is the prepositional phrase “on/onto paper.” بر پیل places the noun ‘elephant’ with the preposition بر (“on/upon/about the elephant” — here marking the subject matter). بنگاشتند is the past plural verb “they inscribed/wrote/laid down.” The verb follows its complements in the classical order: prepositional phrase(s) + verb.
segments.s486-g94: به چشم is literal “to the eye” (i.e. before the sight). جهانجوی is a compound noun “world‑seeker,” here meaning an ambitious or fame‑seeking person whose gaze is being addressed. بگذاشتند is the past plural “they placed/put” and completes the coordinated action with the previous clause: they placed it before the world‑seeker’s eye.
segments.s486-g95: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning “he commanded/ordered.” تا here introduces the content clause (“that / so that”) and is followed by the doers: فیلسوفان روم = “the philosophers of Rome” (subject of the subordinate clause). This is a standard verb‑complement structure with the main verb (order) preceding the subordinate clause marker.
segments.s486-g96: یکی is the indefinite numeral/adjective “one / a certain.” پیل is the object “elephant.” کردند is the past plural “they made/did.” پیشش is the locative “before him / in his presence.” ز + موم is the instrumental/compositional phrase “of/from wax.” The clause literally: “they made one elephant before him of wax.”
segments.s486-g49: This is a protasis introduced by the conditional particle گر ("if"). از خون ما is an ablative/partitive phrase "out of our blood" that gives the material; خاک دریا کنند is a poetic word order meaning literally "make the sea (into) earth" (دریا as the thing being turned into خاک). کنند is 3rd person plural present/subjunctive-like form used for hypothetical action.
segments.s486-g50: نشیبی is the direct object meaning "a slope/embankment"; ز افگنده is a poetic use of ز (= از) with a passive/participle افگنده "that has been cast/spilled" (referring back to the blood) and functions as a material/source phrase. بالا is an adverb/particle meaning "up/upward/high," and کنند repeats the 3rd person plural verb "they make/raise." The whole clause says they would "raise up" an embankment from what was spilled.
segments.s486-g51: نبیند is negative optative/imperative-like third-person form (ن + بیند) often translated "may no one see" or simply "no one should see." کسی here functions as "no one" in this context; پشت ما is a genitive phrase "our rear/back," and روز جنگ is a temporal adjunct "on the day of battle." Note the verb-before-subject word order typical in classical Persian.
segments.s486-g52: اگر introduces a concessive/conditional clause "if/were to". چرخ is the personified sky/heaven (often fate); بار is "load/burden" and آورد is 3rd person singular "brings/should bring." کوه سنگ is a compound noun "mountain-rock" (a large load of stone) serving as the object of آورد. The clause means "even if heaven were to bring a load of mountain-stone."
segments.s486-g53: همه بندگانیم is a simple nominal predicate: همه "all/everyone" + بندگانیم "we are servants/subjects." The coordinated clause و فرمان تراست uses تراست, a poetic contraction of ترا است ("it is yours/for you"), so فرمان تراست = "the command/decree belongs to you."
segments.s486-g54: چو here introduces a conditional sense "if." آزار گیری is a verb phrase with گیری (2nd/imperative/you-form sense "you take/inflict") meaning "if you inflict harm." ز ما is the genitive "from us/our," and جان تراست is literally "the life is yours" (again تراست = ترا است), meaning our lives belong to you. Word order places the conditional clause before the result clause.
segments.s486-g55: چو is temporal "when." بشنید is past tense "he heard." ز ایشان is an ablative "from them/what they said," and سکندر (the subject) and سخن (the speech/words) complete the clause; the verb-before-subject order (بشنید ... سکندر) is a poetic arrangement but means "when Alexander heard their speech."
segments.s486-g56: This clause omits an explicit subject because it was given earlier (سکندر). یکی رزم را places the object "one battle/one combat" with را as the definite/object marker. دیگر is "another/other," and افگند is past tense "threw/cast down." بن ("root/base/foundation") here functions idiomatically as an endpoint/grounding, so the phrase means he "hurled one into battle and cast another down to the ground." The sequence is terse and characteristic of epic compression.
segments.s486-g41: This is an invocation/oath built from coordinated nouns: 'مرایار' is a vocative/epithet and the sequence invokes God and the Iranian host as witnesses ('by God and the army of Iran'). The nouns are presented in a compact, poetic string without additional particles; this phrase functions adverbially to mark the speaker's oath for the following clause.
segments.s486-g42: 'نخواهم' is a first-person negative verb meaning 'I do not/will not want' and introduces a subordinate content clause with 'که'. The clause 'که رومی بود نیک‌خواه' expresses the undesired state; 'بود' is the copula ('be') used in the classical/poetic form, and 'نیک‌خواه' is the predicate noun 'well-wisher' describing 'رومی'.
segments.s486-g43: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). آشفته شد is a two-word predicate 'became disturbed/upset' (adjective + past verb). زان 'from/at that' links the disturbance to the preceding conversation, and گفت و گوی (the compound 'گفت‌وگو') names the talk that caused it.
segments.s486-g44: Here سپه (the army) is the subject; سوی پوزش is a prepositional phrase 'toward pardon/apology'; نهادند is the past plural verb 'they placed/set' (used idiomatically with روی) and روی 'face' is the object. The whole phrase means 'the army turned their faces toward asking pardon.' Word order is typical poetic Persian: subject + directional phrase + verb + object.
segments.s486-g45: که introduces the reported/quoted content. ما (we) + سربسر (an adverb meaning 'utterly/wholly') modifies بنده (servants); قیصریم is an adjectival/nominal form meaning '(we) are of the Caesar' or 'the Caesar's' — together: 'we are wholly the Caesar's servants.' The copula is built into the form قیصریم in classical style.
segments.s486-g46: زمین (the land) is the object; جز introduces the exception ('except'); به فرمان او is an agentive/instrumental phrase 'by his command'; نسپریم is the first-person-plural negative/future-ish verb 'we do not/will not entrust/hand over'. Together: 'we will not hand over the land except by his command.'
segments.s486-g47: بکوشیم is a hortatory/first-person-plural form 'let us strive' or 'we shall strive.' The conjunction و links to a temporal/conditional clause introduced by چون ('when' / 'as'), and اسپ گردد تباه is a predicative clause: اسپ 'the horse/steed', گردد 'becomes' (classical verb form), تباه 'ruined' — 'when the steed becomes ruined.'
segments.s486-g48: پیاده functions adverbially 'on foot'; به جنگ اندر آید is the verbal phrase 'enters into the battle' (به + noun + اندر + آید); سپاه (the army) is the subject, placed after the verb for emphasis as often in classical/epic Persian. The whole gives 'the army will enter the battle on foot.'
segments.s486-g81: به دشت اندرون is a prepositional/locative phrase (به + place + اندرون ‘within’) meaning “into/within the plain.” لشکر انبوه گشت is a subject + adjective + copular verb: لشکر (the host/army) + انبوه (dense, numerous) + گشت (became). گشت is the classical past/copulative verb “became,” coming after the predicative adjective.
segments.s486-g82: زمین is the subject “the ground/earth.” از پی پیل is an adverbial phrase meaning “in the wake of / following the elephant” (از + پی ‘after/following’ + پیل). چون introduces a simile “like,” and کوه گشت is a predicate: کوه (mountain) + گشت (became). Word order is subject — adverbial — simile — verb, with گشت again as the past/copulative.
segments.s486-g83: سپاهی is the plural/collective subject “soldiers/hosts.” کشیدند is past plural of کشیدن used here in the sense “stretched/extended” (they drew out/deployed). بر چار میل is an extent phrase with بر marking span/extent + چار میل “four miles.” The verb is past plural and follows the subject in typical classical word order.
segments.s486-g84: پس functions as a temporal/spatial connector (“then/after” or here ‘at the rear’ in sequence), and پشت گردان is a compound meaning “rear/reserve troops” (پشت ‘rear’ modifying گردان ‘troop/unit’). و links this phrase to در پیش پیل, where در پیش is a locative compound “in front of / before” and پیل is the elephant. The construction lists dispositions: rear units and (in) front the elephant.
segments.s486-g85: ز (poetic form of از) + هندوستان marks origin: “from India.” نیز is an adverb “also,” and کارآگاهان is the plural subject meaning “scouts/investigators/agents” — here ‘scouts’ or ‘reconnoitering men’ in context. The whole phrase names the agents and their origin.
segments.s486-g86: برفتند is past plural of رفتن with the older prefix بر- (here “they went/advanced/proceeded”). نزدیک is a prepositional/adverbial “near/close to,” and شاه جهان is a titulary compound “king of the world” (شاه modified by جهان). The verb is past tense and the locative follows the verb, giving “they advanced close to the king.”
segments.s486-g87: بگفتند is past plural “they said/told.” با او is the indirect object “to/with him” (here “they told him”). بسی is an adverb/adjective meaning “much/many” modifying رزم پیل (رزم + پیل = “battle‑elephants” or “war‑elephants”); بسی is an older/poetic form meaning ‘many’ (sense not listed in the provided options). In sum: “They told him: many war‑elephants…”
segments.s486-g88: که introduces indirect reported content (“that …”). او is the subject “they/he” (referring to the elephants as grammatical subject), اسپ را is the definite object marker construction (“the horse”), and بفگند is an archaic/present‑imperfective form with the prefix ب‑ meaning here “will hurl/throw/crush” (used inside reported speech to indicate what will happen or what they can do). از دو میل expresses the distance/extent “for two miles” (از + numeral + میل).
segments.s486-g113: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past third-person verb meaning 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that'). زان فزون از هزار is an archaic comparative phrase: زان ('of/from that') + فزون ('more/exceeding') + از ('than/from') + هزار ('a thousand'), together meaning 'that which exceeds a thousand' or simply 'more than a thousand.'
segments.s486-g114: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (used before a material). بکردند is the past plural 'they made/did' (b- prefix is common in older/poetic forms). The direct objects اسپ و سوار follow in normal noun-and-noun order: 'horses and riders.'
segments.s486-g115: ازان is an archaic/poetic demonstrative 'of those / from them.' ابرش is a descriptive noun (literally 'its cloud') used here as a color/type (roan/dappled) with the possessive -ش; the following adjectives linked by و list other color/types (خنگ, بور, سیاه). The sequence is a simple enumerative noun phrase 'the roan, kheng, fair, and black.'
segments.s486-g116: که introduces a relative clause ('which/that'). دیدست is an archaic/perfect-form verb ('it appears' / 'he has seen') used here in the third person; word order is inverted (verb before noun) as is common in epic poetry. شاهی functions adjectivally/nominally as 'royal' or 'regal,' and ز آهن سپاه ('an army of iron') is the complement describing what is seen.
segments.s486-g117: از آهن سپاهی is a noun phrase 'an army (سپاهی) made of iron (از آهن).' به گردون براند contains the verb براند (present/subjunctive/habitual form of rاندن 'to drive') with به گردون ('to the sky/heavens') as its goal; the sequence implies '(one) drives/raises an iron army into the sky.'
segments.s486-g118: که introduces a result or restrictive clause ('so that/that'). جز means 'except,' با is the preposition 'with,' and سواران جنگی is a postmodified plural 'war-mounted riders' (سواران 'horsemen' + جنگی 'warlike/military'). نماند is the past/subjunctive negative 'would not remain / remained not,' completing the sense 'so that nothing remained except the war-mounted riders.'
segments.s486-g33: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase meaning “he said thus/so” (چنین = thus + گفت = ‘said’). کز introduces the subordinate clause (“that / that from …”) and governs the following noun phrase جنگ ایرانیان ‘the Iranians’ fighting/battle’ (word order: subordinate marker → noun → possessor).
segments.s486-g34: This clause contains the prepositional marker ز ‘from’ (ز رومی = ‘from / of the Romans’) + کسی را as the (negative) patient, followed by نیامد زیان, literally “harm did not come” (نیامد = past negative ‘did not come’). The object-marker را flags کسی as definite/patient.
segments.s486-g35: به دارا here names (دارا = Darius) as the indirect/affected party; بر … از introduces a comparison (“more/ worse … than”), so the whole phrase reads roughly “a bad thing befell Darius more than (it did) the servants.” رسید is the past tense verb ‘befell/arrived (upon)’.
segments.s486-g36: کسی از شما … ندید is a verbless-looking clause whose main verb is ندید ‘(he) did not see’; کسی از شما (‘anyone / anyone of you’) is the internal object. باد جسته is an archaic/periphrastic verbal cluster: جسته is a past participle (‘having escaped’/‘having sprung away’) and باد functions like an optative/auxiliary; the whole means roughly “(he) saw that no one of you had escaped.”
segments.s486-g37: برین راه = بر + این (fused ‘on/along this road’), with من as the subject; بی‌شما ‘without you’ is an adverbial prepositional phrase. بگذرم is a present/subjunctival form meaning ‘I will pass / I shall go’ (classical Persian often uses such forms for future or hortative sense).
segments.s486-g38: دل اژدها را is the definite object (‘the dragon’s heart’ + object marker را). به پی here means ‘to/into the pursuit / to the pursuers’ (به + پی ‘in/by pursuit’), and بسپرم is the present-subjunctive/first-person verb ‘I will entrust / I will hand over’. The clause means ‘I will entrust the dragon’s heart to the pursuers.’
segments.s486-g39: بیینید is an imperative/jussive (‘see! / observe!’) in the plural or polite form. ازان پس is an adverbial phrase ‘from that time on / afterwards’; که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that …’. رنجور فور = ‘the ailing/sick’ + فور ‘immediately / at once’, so the phrase heads the subordinate clause (‘that the sick immediately …’).
segments.s486-g40: نپردازد is a negative-present/jussive form (ن‑ + پردازد) meaning ‘will not / does not set out / engage’ (from پرداختن in classical usage = ‘to set out, to engage’). از بن = ‘from the base/root’ (بن = foundation/base), and به رزم و به سور = ‘into battle and into festivity’ (رزم = battle, سور = feast). The clause therefore says the sick will not at once go from their base into war or feasting.
segments.s486-g17: نجوید is the verb with a negative prefix ن- plus the present form جوید (here serving as a prohibitive/optative: 'do not seek'). همی is the continuous/emphatic particle 'ever/at once/continually' placed after the verb; جنگ تو is the object 'your war'; فور هند is an adverbial phrase (فور 'immediately') + هند 'India' giving direction/target. The clause thus says 'do not at once seek (pursue) your war against India.'
segments.s486-g18: نه ... نه is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor' linking two noun phrases. فغفور چینی = 'the Chinese faghfur' (a royal/title term), and سالار سند = 'the lord/commander of Sind'; the verb is omitted (elliptical) and the whole phrase is coordinated with the preceding prohibition.
segments.s486-g19: سپه را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the army'). چرا is the interrogative 'why'. کرد is the main verb 'made/caused' (here causative: 'caused/led to'), followed by باید تباه where باید functions as a modal/inevitability marker and تباه 'ruined' expresses the resulting state. Together: 'Why did he cause the army to be ruined.'
segments.s486-g20: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase (ب + دین = به این) meaning 'to/into this'. مرز بی‌ارز = 'this worthless border' (مرز 'border' + بی‌ارز 'worthless'). و زین‌گونه راه = 'and such a road/way' (زین‌گونه 'thus/such' modifying راه 'road'), completing the goal/setting into which the army was led.
segments.s486-g21: ز لشکر is a poetic genitive/ablative 'from the army'. نبینیم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive negative of دیدن 'to see' ('we do not see' / 'we see not'). اسپی درست = 'a proper steed' (اسپی 'horse/steed', درست 'proper'). The clause means 'from the army we see no proper steed.'
segments.s486-g22: که introduces a subordinate clause of consequence/possibility ('that/for/so that'). شاید is a modal adverb 'perhaps/it may be'. به تندی برو = 'may/let (him) go quickly' (به + تندی 'with haste', برو imperative/subjunctive 'go'). رزم جست is a verbal phrase 'spring/leap into battle' where جست (from جستن/جهیدن) means 'to leap' and rزم 'battle' is the target. The clause expresses the possibility that someone will hastily rush into combat.
segments.s486-g23: ازین is 'from this' (از + این, here written از + ین) and جنگ‌گر 'warrior'. بازگردد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive/indicative 'returns/turns back'. سپاه is 'the army' (topic/moved noun). The clause sets the condition 'if this warrior turns back' with سپاه as the affected party.
segments.s486-g24: سوار و پیاده is a coordinated pair 'horse (men) and foot (soldiers)'. نیابند is the negative jussive/subjunctive/indicative of یافتن 'to find' (here 'will not/find no'), and راه 'way/route' is the object. The clause means 'neither the mounted nor the foot troops will find a way.'
segments.s486-g97: چنین گفت is a concise, poetic sequence meaning “he said thus/so”; the verb گفت (past) follows the adverb چنین. کاکنون is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning “now.” به + پاکیزه رای expresses manner/instrument ('with a pure/candid counsel'), where به marks the means and پاکیزه رای is a noun phrase (پاکیزه modifying رای = counsel/opinion).
segments.s486-g98: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / content clause). آرد (from آوردن) here is an optative/subjunctive/imperative-like classical form meaning 'should bring' or 'may bring' (verb before its indefinite subject). یکی functions as an indefinite 'one/a certain' (= 'someone' or 'a single'), چاره این به جای = 'a remedy for this (situation/place)' where به جای literally 'to/for the place' = 'for this matter.'
segments.s486-g99: نشستند is the past tense third-person plural of نشستن meaning 'they sat' or 'they took a seat/gathered'; in poetry the verb can appear before the subject. دانش‌پژوهان is a compound noun 'knowledge-seekers' = 'scholars/students/researchers.' بهم is an adverbial 'together' (they sat together).
segments.s486-g100: یکی چاره جستند = 'they sought a single/a certain remedy' (یکی as 'one/a certain', چاره = remedy, جستند is the past of جستن used here in the sense 'sought'). بر بیش و کم is an adverbial/idiomatic phrase 'regarding more and less' = 'for all degrees / in matters of excess and deficiency' where بر marks the domain and بیش و کم ('more and less') indicate variety or degree.
segments.s486-g101: یکی انجمن کرد = 'he made/held a meeting' (یکی = 'one' or 'a certain' used stylistically, انجمن = 'assembly/meeting', کرد = 'did/made' used as causative/performative 'convened'). ز here is the poetic form of از meaning 'from,' so ز آهنگران = 'from the blacksmiths' (i.e., he summoned blacksmiths).
segments.s486-g102: هرانکس که استاد بود اندران is a relative clause: هر آن کس 'whoever' + که introduces the clause; استاد = 'master/expert'; بود is the past copula 'was'; اندران ('اندر' + ان) = 'among them.' The whole phrase = 'whoever among them was a master.'
segments.s486-g103: ز رومی و از مصری و پارسی lists origins: ز/از both mean 'from' (poetic variation). رومی = people of Rûm (Byzantine/Roman), مصری = Egyptian, پارسی = Persian; the conjunction و links the items. Word order is a simple coordinate list of ethnic/origin adjectives.
segments.s486-g104: فزون بود مرد = 'men were numerous' (فزون = abundant/more, بود = was/were). از چهل بار سی is a numerical expression: از here introduces the excess 'more than,' چهل = forty, بار = 'times' (multiplicative), سی = thirty; thus 'more than forty times thirty' (a classical poetic way to state a large number).
segments.s486-g105: یکی بارگی functions here as a temporal/indefinite phrase ('once' / 'one time') placed before the verb; ساختند is past tense, third-person plural ('they made' / 'they built'), and آهنین is an adjective placed after the verb as a predicate/complement ('iron, made of iron') — a common poetic word order that leaves the precise noun elliptical to be supplied by the next segment.
segments.s486-g106: سوارش is the noun سوار with the third-person possessive enclitic -ش ('his/its rider'); the repeated preposition ز introduces material/composition ('of/from iron') applied to successive items. The line is elliptical and enumerative: 'سوارش ز آهن' = 'his rider of iron', then 'ز آهنش زین' continues in the same pattern to indicate the horse and saddle are likewise of iron. Poetic economy omits some nouns, so 'آهنش' (= 'his iron') stands in for 'his horse made of iron'.
segments.s486-g107: به ... به here marks instrumentality ('with' / 'by means of') before each instrument: 'with nails and with copper'. درزها is the plural object 'the seams', and دوختند is past tense, third-person plural ('they sewed/stitched/fastened'). Word order places the instruments before the object for emphasis, a poetic arrangement.
segments.s486-g108: This clause lists the things sold: 'سوار و تن باره' — two coordinated nouns. سوار is 'the rider', و is 'and', تن 'body', and باره ('بارِه') here means 'load' or 'burden-bearing' so the compound تن باره refers to the body/pack that carries the burden. بفروختند is the past-tense verb 'they sold'.
segments.s486-g109: به گردون is a locative/goal phrase ('to the dome/assembly/sky' — used poetically for the court or hall); براندند is past tense third-person plural ('they drove/led/brought'); بر پیش شاه is a prepositional phrase 'into the presence of the king' (بر + پیش = 'before/in front of' + شاه 'king'). The sequence describes bringing/placing the object before the king.
segments.s486-g110: درونش = درون + enclitic -ش ('its/inside of it'); پر + از = 'full of' (درونش پر از ...). نفط is lamp-oil here (common in older Persian), کرده is the past participle/verb 'made/done' used attributively, and سیاه is the adjective 'black'. Together this reads 'its inside full of lamp-oil, having been made black' (i.e., they had filled and blackened its interior with oil).
segments.s486-g111: سکندر بدید is straightforward past-tense 'Alexander saw'. آن پسند آمدش is an impersonal/explosive construction: پسند آمدن ('to be pleasing') with the dative enclitic -ش meaning 'to him' — literally 'that came pleasing to him', idiomatically 'he liked that'.
segments.s486-g112: خردمند را uses را as a topical/object marker to mark 'the wise man' as the experiencer; سودمند آمدش repeats the same impersonal pattern (آمدن) with -ش: 'it came useful to him' = 'he found it useful' or 'the wise man considered it useful.' The verb form is past-oriented (آمدش).
segments.s486-g73: بدان is an archaic/poetic compound (ب + دان) functioning like an imperative/resultive marker 'know/so that'; تا introduces the result/purpose clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). پسِ پشت او = 'his rear/behind him' (پس + پشت, 'پس' here = 'behind/after'). زین گروه is a noun phrase 'this band/troop' (زین is an older/dialectal demonstrative here; it links the group to what will follow). Together the segment sets up: 'so that, behind him, this group...'.
segments.s486-g74: در و دشت are coordinated location nouns ('...the countryside and the plain'); the verb گردد (third person singular, present/subjunctive/optative form of گشتن/گردیدن) expresses the resulting change 'become/turn into'. به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into the manner/likeness of', and کوه is the comparison noun 'mountain'. The whole clause: '...the plain and countryside become like a mountain.'
segments.s486-g75: از ... و از ... is a repeated prepositional marker 'from/among', listing two sources. اخترشناسان and موبدان are the two groups named ('astrologers/astronomers' and 'mōbeds, Zoroastrian priests'). The construction means 'from the astrologers and from the mobeds...'.
segments.s486-g76: جهاندیده and نامور are adjectives before the noun بخردان; in classical Persian adjectives commonly precede the substantive. بخردان (بخرد + ان) is a plural substantive 'the wise ones' (wise/learned men). Together: 'the worldly-wise and renowned wise men' drawn from the named groups.
segments.s486-g77: همی is a classical habitual/continuous particle (indicating repeated or ongoing action, 'kept/was taking'). برد here is the verb 'took/led' (classical past/present usage of بردن); با خویشتن = 'with himself' i.e. 'with him'. شست مرد = 'sixty men' (شست is the archaic numeral 'sixty', placed before the noun مرد). The clause means 'he took with him sixty men.'
segments.s486-g78: پژوهنده is an agentive/deverbal noun 'one who investigates' or 'inquirer/observer'; here it modifies the men just mentioned. روزگار نبرد is a genitive chain 'the time of battle' (روزگار = 'time/occasion', نبرد = 'battle'). Together the phrase describes those sixty men as 'investigators of the times of battle' (i.e. men who study/choose the auspicious time for battle).
segments.s486-g79: چو + آگاه شد = 'when he became aware/informed' (چو = 'when'); فور is an adverb 'immediately'; کامد (archaic form of آمدن) = 'came/arrived'. سپاه is the subject 'the army'. The sequence gives: 'When he learned (of it), immediately the army came.'
segments.s486-g80: گزین کرد is a compound verbal expression 'chose/selected' (گزین + کرد). جای is the object 'a place/position'. از در رزمگاه is a locative expression literal 'from the gate/entrance of the battlefield' (از + در (here 'door/gate') + رزمگاه 'battlefield'), idiomatically 'at/by the field's entrance' or 'a place in the battle-line'. So: 'he selected a place at the entrance of the battlefield.'
segments.s486-g25: چو here is a temporal subordinating conjunction 'when/while' (not simile). پیروز بودیم is a past-tense clause 'we were victorious' (بودیم = past plural 'we were'). تا این زمان is an adverbial 'up to this time/until now' modifying the temporal span introduced by چو.
segments.s486-g26: به هرجای is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'to/at every place/wherever'. بر لشگر بدگمان is predicative: بدگمان is an adjective 'suspicious' that takes the preposition بر + object (لشگر) meaning 'suspicious of the army'; the copula is implied or understood from the earlier clause (elliptical linking of predicates is common in classical Persian).
segments.s486-g27: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. سربه‌سر is an adverbial intensifier 'entirely/throughout' that here scopes over 'کوه و دریا' ('mountain and sea alike'). به پیش means 'ahead/before (them)' and locates the scene in front of the subject.
segments.s486-g28: به سیری is an adverbial phrase 'to/for satiation' (here idiomatically meaning 'completely/safely'); نیامد is the negative past 'did not come/return'; کس functions as an indefinite/negative subject 'no one' with this negated verb; از جان خوی ش (از جان خویش) literally 'from his life' = 'with his life intact' (classical idiom).
segments.s486-g29: مگردان is a negative imperative 'do not turn/convert' addressing someone; همه نام ما را is the definite object 'our whole name' with را marking the object; به ننگ is a prepositional result-state 'into shame/disgrace' (marks the result of turning).
segments.s486-g30: نکردست is an archaic perfective/formal negative 'has not done' (third-person singular); the verb precedes the subject کس here (verb–subject order). جنگ با آب و سنگ is the object/phrase 'war/fight with water and stone', where با marks the instrument/partner ('with'), emphasizing the triviality of the opponents.
segments.s486-g31: غمی شد is a copular-like construction 'became sorrowful' (غمی = grief, شد = 'became'); سکندر is the subject. ز introduces the cause 'because of/from' and گفتارشان 'their words' is the noun of cause (ز + noun).
segments.s486-g32: برآشفت is a prefixed past verb 'he became angry/was agitated' (بر- verbal prefix + آشفَت); و is the coordinating 'and'; بشکست is a past verb 'he broke/smash(ed)' and بازارشان 'their bazaar/market' is the object (possessive suffix -شان). The sequence gives two successive actions by the subject.

sentences.s486-s29: He selected thirty thousand from the Iranians who were equipped for battle.
sentences.s486-s30: Forth marched the seasoned, armored leaders — men and warriors.
sentences.s486-s31: Then behind them rode Roman horsemen — one corps after another, in all the same forty thousand.
sentences.s486-s32: Then behind them rode the Egyptian horsemen: the brave and the dagger-bearers of Egypt.
sentences.s486-s1: When the reply reached Alexander, he immediately picked out leaders from the army.
sentences.s486-s2: so that they would be worthy and foremost, accomplished in ancient learning and new in years.
sentences.s486-s3: He at once sent an Indian army in that direction, so that no land remained except the sea.
sentences.s486-s4: The army went off in every direction like that; you said there was no path on the land except that.
sentences.s486-s33: Forty thousand swordsmen went forth, every man who belonged to the field of battle.
sentences.s486-s34: From Dara's kinsmen and the Iranians, whoever was of the Kian lineage.
sentences.s486-s35: From the Romans, from the Egyptians, and the Berbers — worthy riders and troops.
sentences.s486-s36: The Caesar chose twelve thousand — all combatants and all famed.
sentences.s486-s5: The fire in the warriors' hearts laid waste to every mountain, sea, and broad road.
sentences.s486-s6: Because the army marched, the whole host everywhere grew weary from that difficult road and the fierce fighting.
sentences.s486-s7: At that moment, when the army reached its encampment, a group advanced close to the king.
sentences.s486-s8: O Caesar of Rome and lord of China — may the earth not bear your army.
sentences.s486-s45: No rider could come upon him; once that happened there was no way to retreat.
sentences.s486-s46: For his trunk rose above the air; from the heavens, Saturn was his helper.
sentences.s486-s47: They inscribed (it) on paper about the elephant; they set it before the eye of the world‑seeker.
sentences.s486-s48: He commanded that the philosophers of Rome make an elephant for him out of wax.
sentences.s486-s25: If they were to turn the sea into soil with our blood, they would heap up a slope from what was spilled and raise it upward.
sentences.s486-s26: May no one see our backs on the day of battle, even if the heavens were to bring a load of mountain-rock.
sentences.s486-s27: We are all servants and the command is yours; if you inflict harm, our lives are (at) your disposal.
sentences.s486-s28: When Alexander heard their words, he sent one into battle and hurled another to the ground.
sentences.s486-s21: By God and by the army of Iran, I will not have a Roman as a well-wisher.
sentences.s486-s22: When the king became upset at that conversation, he ended the talk, and the army turned their faces toward asking pardon.
sentences.s486-s23: That we are entirely the Caesar's servants; we will not hand over the land except by his command.
sentences.s486-s24: We will strive, and when the steed is ruined, the army will enter the battle on foot.
sentences.s486-s41: The host crowded into the plain; the earth, following the elephant, became like a mountain.
sentences.s486-s42: Soldiers stretched out across four miles; behind them were the rear troops, and before them the elephant.
sentences.s486-s43: Scouts from India also advanced close to the king of the world.
sentences.s486-s44: They told him that there were many battle‑elephants, and that they would hurl a horse two miles.
sentences.s486-s57: He commanded that, of them, more than a thousand be made into iron horses and riders.
sentences.s486-s58: Among them were the roan, the kheng, the fair, and the black — an iron host that the king had seen.
sentences.s486-s59: He could raise an army of iron to the heavens, so that nothing would remain except the war-mounted riders.
sentences.s486-s17: He said that in the Iranians' fighting no Roman suffered any loss.
sentences.s486-s18: A worse fate befell Darius than his bad servants; I saw none of you who had escaped.
sentences.s486-s19: I will go this way without you; I will leave the dragon’s heart to the pursuers.
sentences.s486-s20: See — from that time on the ailing will not immediately set out from their base into battle and into feasting.
sentences.s486-s9: Do not at once seek war against India; neither the Chinese faghfur nor the lord of Sind.
sentences.s486-s10: Why did he doom the army to ruin, bringing it into this worthless border and on such a road?
sentences.s486-s11: From the army we see no proper steed, for perhaps he will quickly go and spring into battle.
sentences.s486-s12: If this warrior turns back, the army — horse and foot — will find no way.
sentences.s486-s49: He said thus: now, with a pure/candid counsel, that someone should bring a remedy for this matter.
sentences.s486-s50: The scholars sat together; they sought a single remedy for more and less.
sentences.s486-s51: He convened a council of blacksmiths — whoever among them was a master.
sentences.s486-s52: They came from Rûm and from Egypt and from Persia; the men were more than forty times thirty (i.e., over twelve hundred).
sentences.s486-s53: Once they fashioned an iron one; his rider was of iron, and his horse and his saddle were of iron.
sentences.s486-s54: They stitched (or riveted) the seams with nails and with copper; they sold both the rider and the load-bearing body (the pack/body with its burden).
sentences.s486-s55: They brought it into the hall and set it before the king; its interior had been filled with lamp-oil and made black.
sentences.s486-s56: Alexander saw it and liked it; the wise man considered it useful.
sentences.s486-s37: So that behind him this band made the plain and countryside turn into the likeness of a mountain.
sentences.s486-s38: From among the astrologers and from the mobeds, the worldly-wise and famed wise men.
sentences.s486-s39: He took with him sixty men, investigators of the times of battle.
sentences.s486-s40: When he was informed, immediately the army arrived; he chose a position at the entrance of the battlefield.
sentences.s486-s13: While we were victorious up to that time, we were suspicious of the army wherever it went.
sentences.s486-s14: Now, with mountains and seas alike before them, no one returned with his life intact.
sentences.s486-s15: Do not turn our whole name to shame; no one has fought a battle with mere water and stones.
sentences.s486-s16: Alexander was grieved by their words; he grew angry and smashed their market.
